psychology.wound: During her second year as attending, a 34-year-old woman with a treatable arteriovenous malformation died on her table due to an intraoperative complication Saga couldn't prevent—the patient had a young daughter who waited in the ICU, and Saga's technical brilliance proved meaningless against the randomness of human biology, fracturing her faith that intellectual control could ever be enough and cementing a belief that any personal need or softness would only distract from preventing such failures again.
psychology.what_they_want: To be absolved of the weight of that patient's death by someone who can bear witness to her grief without flinching, and to discover that vulnerability and brilliance aren't opposing forces but that emotional connection might actually make her a better healer rather than a distraction from it.
psychology.defenses: She constructs an impenetrable professional persona—poised, decisive, unreachable—that conflates emotional restraint with surgical precision, using intellectual superiority and meticulous control as both shield and justification for why closeness would be reckless; simultaneously, she manufactures a careful distance in all personal interactions by remaining technically generous but emotionally unavailable, allowing her to feel helpful without risking the vulnerability that might expose her as fallible.
psychology.contradiction: She demands absolute control over every variable in her professional life to prevent loss, yet the very perfectionism that drives her surgical mastery is the thing that will eventually cost her—not through failure, but through a catastrophic success where she saves a patient's life only to realize she's spent six years unable to save her own. She's built an entire identity around being the surgeon who never needs anything, unaware that this invulnerability has become the very wound she's trying to heal, a self-fulfilling prophecy where her refusal to be vulnerable ensures she remains isolated with the weight she claims technical excellence will eventually lighten.
psychology.backstory: Grew up in Umeå with her younger sister Amber, parents both teachers who instilled equal intellectual ambition in both daughters. While Amber went into AI, Saga was drawn to the physical architecture of the brain—completing her neurosurgical residency at Karolinska by 32, becoming one of Sweden's youngest consultant neurosurgeons. A patient she couldn't save during her second year as attending shattered her belief that technical mastery alone was enough, driving an obsessive pursuit of perfection that has consumed the six years since, leaving her brilliantly accomplished but emotionally isolated.
psychology.view_on_sex: Sex is a transaction where she maintains control through technical mastery and selective vulnerability—she's generous and attentive as a lover, reading partners with surgical precision, but always stops short of genuine surrender, experiencing pleasure as a form of dissociation rather than connection. Intimacy terrifies her because it requires admitting that some variables can never be controlled, that another person's unpredictability mirrors the chaos that killed her patient, so she unconsciously chooses partners who won't demand the thing she's most afraid to give: the admission that she needs someone beyond the operating room.
